coredata.dc:title: Synergistic role of carbon quantum dots in the activity and stability of Candida rugosa lipase encapsulated within metal–organic frameworks (ZIF-8)
coredata.dc:creator: Ozyilmaz, Elif
Caglar, Ozge
Sargin, Idris
Arslan, Gulsin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are particularly attractive in biosensing, bioimaging and drug delivery applications due to their luminescent properties, water dispersibility and favorable biocompatibility. In the study, by combining CQDs derived from edible mushroom Agaricus bisporus and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) crystals, a new support material was prepared for encapsulation of Candida rugosa lipase. The study aimed to shed light on the activity, thermal and storage stability, and reusability of physically adsorbed or in-situ encapsulated enzyme. The enzyme loading efficiency of the in-situ encapsulation method was 3.11-fold higher (371 mg g−1). Also, catalytic activities were 119.8 U/g (CQDs@ZIF- 8@CRLenc) and 34.2 U/g (CQDs@ZIF-8@CRLads). The CQDs@ZIF-8@CRLenc with the biocatalytic activity was 3.5-folds higher than that of the CQDs@ZIF-8@CRLads. In the in-situ encapsulation, the CQDs and the enzyme were physically entrapped within three-dimensional structures of ZIF-8 that were constructed from tetrahedral zinc ions bridged by imidazolate. The structural, morphological, and spectrophotometric analyses were performed using TEM, SEM-EDX, FT-IR, XRD, UV–vis, fluorescence, and confocal microscopy. The effects of pH (4.0–9.0) and temperature (30–60 °C) on the enzymatic activity were studied and optimized. The coexistence of the CQDs and ZIF-8 crystals remarkably enhanced the activity, reusability, thermal resistance, and storage stability of the enzyme encapsulated within ZIF-8 crystals (the relative enzyme activity; after 6 re-uses: 73%, 2-hour-incubation at 60 °C: 70%, 5-week-storage at 4 °C: 70%). The CQDs from A. bisporus entrapped within ZIF-8 can synergistically enhance the catalytic performance and thermal/storage stability of immobilized C. rugosa lipase.

Fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solutions of CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 (λex of CQDs = 330 nm and λex of CQDs@ZIF-8 340 = 340 nm). The fluorescence emission of CQDs@ZIF-8 indicates that the CQDs were encapsulated within ZIF-8.

head.ce:title: Synergistic role of carbon quantum dots in the activity and stability of Candida rugosa lipase encapsulated within metal–organic frameworks (ZIF-8)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are particularly attractive in biosensing, bioimaging and drug delivery applications due to their luminescent properties, water dispersibility and favorable biocompatibility. In the study, by combining CQDs derived from edible mushroom Agaricus bisporus and zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) crystals, a new support material was prepared for encapsulation of Candida rugosa lipase. The study aimed to shed light on the activity, thermal and storage stability, and reusability of physically adsorbed or in-situ encapsulated enzyme. The enzyme loading efficiency of the in-situ encapsulation method was 3.11-fold higher (371 mg g−1). Also, catalytic activities were 119.8 U/g (CQDs@ZIF- 8@CRLenc) and 34.2 U/g (CQDs@ZIF-8@CRLads). The CQDs@ZIF-8@CRLenc with the biocatalytic activity was 3.5-folds higher than that of the CQDs@ZIF-8@CRLads. In the in-situ encapsulation, the CQDs and the enzyme were physically entrapped within three-dimensional structures of ZIF-8 that were constructed from tetrahedral zinc ions bridged by imidazolate. The structural, morphological, and spectrophotometric analyses were performed using TEM, SEM-EDX, FT-IR, XRD, UV–vis, fluorescence, and confocal microscopy. The effects of pH (4.0–9.0) and temperature (30–60 °C) on the enzymatic activity were studied and optimized. The coexistence of the CQDs and ZIF-8 crystals remarkably enhanced the activity, reusability, thermal resistance, and storage stability of the enzyme encapsulated within ZIF-8 crystals (the relative enzyme activity; after 6 re-uses: 73%, 2-hour-incubation at 60 °C: 70%, 5-week-storage at 4 °C: 70%). The CQDs from A. bisporus entrapped within ZIF-8 can synergistically enhance the catalytic performance and thermal/storage stability of immobilized C. rugosa lipase.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frames (MOFs) are assembly of metal ions that are coordinated to organic ligands. MOFs are highly crystalline porous materials with attractive properties like high surface area, tuneable ultra-high porosity, engineered functionality, and excellent thermal stability [1,2]. Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) constitute a subfamily of MOFs. ZIFs exhibit favorable properties like crystallinity, microporosity, large surface area, rich structural diversity, and exceptional thermal and chemical stability for wide range of applications [3–5]. However, physically, or chemically unmodified MOF materials generally cannot meet complex requirements of diverse applications. Thus, various nanoparticle-MOF composites have been engineered with the desirable structural properties that either component cannot offer alone [6].

Mostly, a single MOF material cannot meet all the requirements needed for a specific application. Thus, preparation of quantum dots-MOF composites has attracted recent interest and has become a new research area. Quantum dots (QDs) of small size and high surface energy tend to coalesce, resulting in losses in the performance of the composite material. The combination of QDs and MOF can increase the dispersion and stability of QDs. Also, QDs-MOF composite has the advantages of both QDs and MOF compounds, and the resulting materials can exhibit synergistic effects [7].

Photoluminescent CQDs are carbon-based nanostructures with exceptional properties like excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission, solubility in aqueous solutions and low toxicity. Photoluminescence of CQDs is mainly attributed to the bandgap transitions of conjugated sp2-hybrized domains and the surface defects. CQDs generally have crystalline graphite-like cores and amorphous domains in their structures. CQDs’ surface consists of oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur-bearing functionalities, and this makes them soluble in aqueous solutions and suitable for physicochemical modifications. Carbon constitutes a high portion of CQDs and is found in the form of crystalline graphite-like structures in the core. CQDs also have a relatively small amount of oxygen, hydrogen, and nitrogen as carboxyl, hydroxyl, or amino functionalities. The hydrophilic and biocompatible properties of CQDs are generally attributed to these polar functional. Chemical modifications of CQDs are also done through the functional groups of CQDs’ surfaces [7,8].

CQDs are particularly attractive in biological applications for which toxicity risks pose challenges. Thus, CQDs are widely applied in cell and tissue imaging, gene and drug delivery and biosensing of biological molecules [8–10]. For instance, in a recent study by Atchudan et al., (2019), nitrogen-doped CQDs were used as a fluorophore agent for in vivo multicolor imaging of Caenorhabditis elegans. The nitrogen-doped CQDs showed remarkable biocompatibility and were used for high-contrast imaging of living and dead C. elegans [11]. In an earlier study by Pierrat et al., (2015), cationic CQDs were synthesized through microwave radiation of citric acid and bPEI25k. As-prepared CQDs were used as nanocarriers to deliver plasmid DNA or small interfering RNA in various cell lines [12]. In a more recent study, red-emissive, doxorubicin-loaded CQDs capable of entering the nuclei of cancer cells and cancer stem cells were used for destruction of cancer cells [13]. Luminescent CQDs have also a great potential as a low-toxic fluorophore probe. Buk and Pemble (2019) designed a miniaturized glucose sensor by immobilizing glucose oxidase onto CQDs-Au nanoparticles [14].

CQDs are also being used as components of the electrodes used for detection of enzymatic activity due to their outstanding photostability, tuneable emission and biocompatibility [15]. The studies mainly focused on the use of CQDs as a component in an electrode system for detection of glucose [16,17], ascorbic acid [18] and ethynylestradiol [19] or as enzyme mimics [20]. The literature reports on the interaction of CQDs with enzymes are limited in number, but the findings are appealing and interesting [21]. For instance, bright fluorescent N-doped carbon dots were synthesized and encapsulated into ZIF-8 by solvothermal method. The encapsulated composites (N-CDs@ZIF-8) retained their photoluminescence properties in the solid state [22]. However, there are no literature reports on immobilization of enzymes and CQDs within MOFs.

Enzymes are widely used in various industrial products and processes including pharmaceuticals, fuel production and food processing [23–26]. Lipases (EC 3.1.1.3) are one of the most important industrial biocatalysts and is used to catalyze many different reactions including hydrolysis, esterification, transesterification, alcoholysis, acidolysis and aminolysis [27–33]. The Candida rugosa lipase has an active site consisting of 31 amino acids with a lid having a catalytic triad of glutamine, histidine and serine (Glu-His-Ser) [29–34]. The inner surface of lid is hydrophilic, and the outer surface is hydrophobic and can have two different conformations, open and closed [29,35,36]. CRL carry out their catalytic actions at the substrate–water interface of heterogeneous reaction systems [29,37]. Usually, the open form of lipases occurs with the movement of the lid in the presence of hydrophobic surfaces, increasing the enzyme activity [38].

The problems faced in the industrial lipase applications often stem from low operational stability and reusability of the industrial lipases [39,40]. Therefore, many efforts are being made to devise immobilization techniques to improve the performance of industrial lipases [41–49]. The most common immobilization techniques are physical or chemical [50]. In the new technologies, the five main immobilization techniques are still used [50]: adsorption [51], encapsulation [52], covalent bonding [53–57], entrapment [58], and crosslinking [59].

In present study, we used adsorption and encapsulation methods. One of the strategies for immobilizing the enzyme on the surface of zeolites is adsorption. This procedure is beneficial since it is simple, quick, and free of harmful substances, as well as having high repeatability [60,61]. Adsorption strength is determined by the affinity of the enzyme protein for the molecular sieve surface [62]. In zeolites, this strength can be adjusted by changing the composition or surface properties of the zeolite [63].

Encapsulation preserves the conformational structure of the enzyme by entirely embedding it in the supports without allowing it to interact with the external interface. Therefore, it has proven to be advantageous over other methods [45,64]. Therefore, in this study, two different immobilized enzymes were obtained by using encapsulation and adsorption methods in enzyme immobilization under ambient conditions, which is a fast, easy and low-cost process [64].

Considering the unique properties and mesoporous structure of MOFs, it has been stated that when used as a support material in the encapsulation method in enzyme immobilization, it provides advantages in improving the catalytic performance of enzymes even under harsh reaction conditions [1,65–67].

Recent findings have revealed that MOFs-based bio-composites possess a great potential for industrial bio-catalysis applications in which devising new strategies to enhance enzyme stability is of significance [34,41,68,69].

In this study it was hypothesized that fluorescent CQDs derived from an edible mushroom Agaricus bisporus can be used as a nano-sized support for Candida rugosa lipase molecules. CQDs with lipase molecules can be physically confined within ZIF-8. The framework around the enzymes not only improves catalytic activity by stabilizing the catalytic conformation of the enzymes, but it also renders them mechanically and thermally stable. In addition, hierarchical porosity and structures give a wide surface area, which helps MOF encapsulate enzymes more effectively [70,71]. Coexistence of CQDs can enhance activity, thermal and storage stability, and reusability of lipase enzyme that has been encapsulated in CQDs-ZIF-8 system.

In this study, lipase-CQDs-MOF systems (lipase-CQDs@ZIF-8) were prepared using two different techniques i.e., physical adsorption and in-situ encapsulation. The CQDs-MOFs composites were characterized using different techniques (SEM, SEM-EDX, TEM, FTIR, XRD, confocal microscopy and UV–vis spectrophotometry) and used as supports to immobilize Candida rugosa lipase (commercial, Sigma L1754). The synergistic effects of CQDs and ZIF-8 on the activity, reusability, thermal resistance, and storage stability of the enzyme were examined.

C. rugosa lipase (CRL, type VII, L1754 Sigma, protein content of free lipase loading 9%), 2-Methylimidazole, zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O), p-nitrophenyl palmitate (p-NPP), Bradford reagent were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich·H2O2 solution (30%) was obtained from Merck.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CQDs from edible mushroom A. bisporus
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were synthesized through microwave irradiation of edible mushroom powder (Agaricus bisporus) in hydrogen peroxide solution. The synthesis and physicochemical, structural and morphologic characterization of CQDs were detailed and discussed in an earlier report [72]. In that study CQDs from A. bisporus was used as TiO2 sensitizers for photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. In present study CQDs from A. bisporus were used for immobilization of C. rugosa lipase in zeolitic imidazolate frameworks. Here, the synthesis of CQDs from A. bisporus can be described briefly as follows: Dried and powdered A. bisporus sample (5.0 g) in 50 mL hydrogen peroxide solution (15% by volume) was irradiated in a microwave oven for 7 min (the power level of the microwave instrument: 400 W). The crude solution was centrifugated for 30 min to remove mushroom debris (at 3260 x g). The supernatant was collected and then filtrated with a syringe filter (Millex-GP Syringe Filter Unit, 0.22 µm, Millipore Co., Cork, Ireland). The filtrate was then transferred into a dialysis tubing (Viskase Sales Corp., Seamless Cellulose Tubing, Size: 16/32, Lot: 208001). The CQDs solution was dialyzed against distilled water for 24 h. The dialysis medium was refreshed for several times. Then, the aqueous solution of CQDs was freeze-dried. The dried CQD sample was kept in an amber container in the dark (The reader can refer to the paper [72] for HR-TEM images, XRD pattern, FT-IR, XPS, UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra analyses of CQDs and their corresponding discussions).

ZIF-8 was synthesized by following the method reported elsewhere [34,73]. The synthesis procedure is detailed as follows. 2-Methylimidazole (1.05 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water by stirring on a magnetic stirrer (at 200 rpm). Zn(NO3)2·6H2O solution (5.0 mL, 92.5 mg mL−1) was added dropwise into the 2-Methylimidazole solution under constant stirring (10 min at 200 rpm). Following the addition of zinc solution, the mixture was further stirred for 30 min until it was turned into milky color. The mixture was kept at 4 °C overnight. Finally, the mixture was centrifuged and rinsed with deionized water for three times and lyophilized.

CQDs@ZIF-8 was prepared as follows: ZIF-8 (0.01 g) was mixed with 4.0 mL of CQD solution (5 mg mL−1). The CQD solution was prepared in 50 mM phosphate buffered saline, PBS, pH 7.0 and stirred at room temperature for 120 min (at 200 rpm). After that, the mixture was rested at 4 °C overnight. Then, the mixture was centrifuged and rinsed with deionized water for three times. Finally, the product was lyophilized. All the assays were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C. rugosa lipase immobilization in CQDs@ZIF-8 system
ce:section.ce:para: For C. rugosa lipase immobilization two methods were used: In the first procedure, lipase molecules and CQDs were physically attached onto the support ZIF-8. In the second procedure, CQDs and the enzyme molecules were physically confined within the ZIF-8. Each procedure is described as follows:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD@ZIF-8@CRL by physical adsorption
ce:section.ce:para: ZIF-8 (0.01 g) was added into the enzyme solution (5 mg mL−1, 20 mL PBS, pH 7.0) and stirred for 10 min (at 150 rpm at room temperature). Subsequently, CQD solution (5 mg mL−1, 4.0 mL PBS, pH 7.0) was added into the mixture and stirred for another 120 min. Upon keeping at 4 °C in a fridge overnight, the mixture was centrifuged to collect ZIF-8 particles. The particles were rinsed with deionized water three times and then lyophilized to dryness. The resulting product was called as CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads. All the assays were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQD@ZIF-8@CRL by in-situ encapsulation
ce:section.ce:para: 2-Methylimidazole (1.05 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and then mixed with CQD solution (5 mg mL−1, 4.0 mL PBS, pH 7.0) under constant stirring (at 200 rpm for 10 min at room temperature). After the addition of the enzyme solution (5 mg mL−1, 20 mL PBS, pH:7) and Zn(NO3)2·6H2O (92.5 mg mL−1, 1.0 mL), the mixture was stirred for 120 min (at 150 rpm at room temperature). After that, the mixture was incubated at 4 °C overnight in a fridge. Following the incubation, the mixture was centrifuged and rinsed with deionized water for three times and lyophilized to dryness. The resulting product was called as CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc. All the assays were performed in triplicate.

The typical enzymatic activity of free CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc was assayed following the method based on p-nitrophenyl palmitate hydrolysis reaction [34,74]. Certain amount (10 mg) of CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc was added into p-NPP solution (14.4 mM, 1.0 mL) and stirred at room temperature for 5 min. Then, the reaction was terminated by addition of Na2CO3 (0.025 M, 2 mL) solution. The reaction medium was centrifuged (4000 rpm) and the supernatant was assayed for the hydrolysis product (p-nitrophenol: p-NP) by measuring the absorbance on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (at λ = 405 nm). Protein assay was performed for the filtrate according to the Bradford Method [75]. Each enzymatic assay was done in triplicate.

The enzymatic activity assays were also conducted at varying pHs (4.0–9.0) (PBS buffers) at room temperature. The enzyme solution (i.e., CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc) was incubated for 15 min. Then, the enzymatic activity was assayed as described above.

The enzymatic activity assays were also conducted at temperature range of 30–60 °C (in PBS buffer). The enzyme solution, (i.e., CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc) was incubated for 15 min. Then, the enzymatic activity was assayed as described above.

The enzyme solution, free or immobilized, (i.e., CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc) was incubated at 60 °C for 20–120 min. Then, the enzymatic activity was assayed as described.

The reusability tests for ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc were conducted as described in Section 2.2.5. The tests were repeated 6 times. Following each enzymatic hydrolysis test, the immobilized enzymes (ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc) were recovered by centrifugation (4000 rpm) and rinsed with PBS (pH: 7.0) and reused. Lipase activity test was done as described.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Storage stability of ZIF-8@CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8@CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8@CRLens
ce:section.ce:para: In the storage stability tests, the immobilized lipase (ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLens) were conducted as follows. CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLens in PBS (pH: 7.0) were kept at 4 °C for 5 weeks. The activity assays were repeated at one-week intervals and done as described.

The Michaelis constant (Km) and maximum reaction rate (Vmax) of free CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads, and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc were measured using p-NPP at room temperature. Kinetic parameters (Vmax and Km) were determined.

Structural, morphological, and spectrophotometric analyses of ZIF-8, CQDs, CQDs@ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-8ads and CQDs@ZIF-8enc were performed using the following analytical tools.
a)
Particle size and morphological analysis was done using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, JEOL, JSM5310, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL JEM-2100, UHR–Ultra High-Resolution instrument) and confocal microscopy (Nikon A1R1 confocal microscope).
b)
X-ray diffractograms (XRD) were obtained using Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer; 2θ angle was scanned between 0° and 50° at a scan rate of 3° min−1.
c)
Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Bruker Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrometer in the range of 4000 and 500 cm−1.
d)
Surface distribution of elements was studied using EDX analysis (SEM, JEOL, JSM5310, Japan).
e)
Ultraviolet-visible (UV–vis) absorbance spectra were obtained on Shimadzu UV-1700 Pharma UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer.
f)
Fluorescence intensity of CQDs was measured on Perkin Elmer LS 55 instrument Perkin Elmer, Cambridge, UK.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of CQDs from edible mushroom A. bisporus
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs from edible mushroom A. bisporus used in this study were synthesized in an earlier work in which CQDs were used as TiO2 sensitizers for photocatalytic hydrogen production [72]. Detailed structural, morphological and photophysical analysis of CQDs from A. bisporus and the corresponding discussions were reported in that publication. Here, the characterization studies of CQDs from A. bisporus were summarized for the sake of clarity as follows.

The TEM images revealed that CQDs (~20 nm) were spherical and had graphite-like crystalline cores with crystal lattice fringes. One of the TEM micrographs (not reported earlier) is presented here ( Fig. 1). The crystalline nature of CQDs was also shown by XRD analysis. The X-ray diffractogram showed that CQDs had both crystalline and amorphous domains (the peak at 2θ = 16° was attributed to amorphous carbon and the peaks at 2θ = 22 and 26° to the presence of amorphous carbon). In FTIR spectrum of CQDs the stretching vibrations were recorded for –O–H, N–H, CC, C–O, –CH2, C–H and S–C bonds. The XPS spectrum analysis revealed that CQDs mainly consisted of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen atoms. Deconvolutions of C 1 s, O 1 s and N 1 s signals in the XPS survey spectrum were as follows. C 1 s: CC, C–O, C–N; O 1 s: CO, C–O–C, C–OH and N 1 s: CN–C, C–N–C. The UV–vis absorption of CQDs showed π–π * transition of conjugated CC units at 217 nm and a broad absorbance peak to 600 nm, which was ascribed to multiple energy absorbing functionalities on the CQDs' surface. The excitation of aqueous solution of the CQDs at 345 nm led to the highest emission peak at 427 nm. The aqueous solutions of CQDs had excitation-dependent fluorescence emission in the range of 305–425 nm. Excitation at 425 nm produced the maximum emission peak at 506 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of ZIF-8 and immobilized lipases
ce:section.ce:para: ZIFs consist of tetrahedral networks where Zn2+ ions are coordinated to imidazole derivatives. In the study ZIF-8 was synthesized following the procedure reported in an earlier paper [34]. In typical procedure ZIF-8 is formed in aqueous solution of Zn2+ ions and 2-mIM at room temperature.

Enzymes are generally immobilized onto the external surfaces of a solid support or encapsulated in a porous material [76]. In this study, two different immobilization methods i.e., physical adsorption and encapsulation were used to immobilize CRL. CRL was used as a model enzyme ( Scheme 1). Surface immobilization was relatively easier method for physical adsorption of CRL onto CQDs@ZIF-8 surface. The CRL adsorption involved molecular and electrostatic interactions of lipase molecules through physical weak interactions. In the adsorption procedure the synthesized ZIF-8 was interacted with CQDs and lipase [77].

In in-situ encapsulation procedure, CQDs and the enzyme molecules were entrapped within the ZIF-8 crystals during the synthesis of ZIF-8. Zn2+ ions, 2-Methylimidazole, CQDs and the enzyme were present in the reaction medium. Two components, i.e., Zn2+ ions and 2-Methylimidazole molecules, formed 3-D structures through coordinate bonds in the presence of CQDs and the enzyme molecules. The ZIF-8 s that had formed in the reaction medium formed protective coatings for CQDS and lipase molecules (Scheme 1).

In recent years, hybrid CQDs/MOFs materials have been applied as photocatalysts, biosensors for luminescence detection and drug carriers [17,23,78]. However, the number of the literature reports on enzyme immobilization on CQDs-ZIF-8 structures is limited.

In the study, the enzyme loading through the in-situ encapsulation procedure was more efficient. The enzyme loading on ZIF-8 in the physical adsorption method (CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads) was recorded as 119 mg g−1. On the other hand, it was 371 mg g−1 for in-situ encapsulation method (CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc). The loading efficiency of the encapsulation method was 3.11-fold higher as compared with the physical adsorption method. The results showed that lipase activity of CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads were obtained to be 119.8 Units/g and 34.2 Units/g, respectively. The lipase activity data of CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc was 3.5 times higher than that of the CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads. In addition, the specific activities of the immobilized enzymes (CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc) were calculated. These values were found as 0.283 U/mg and 0.323 U/mg, respectively. In the physical adsorption method, the enzyme immobilization occurred through the weak interactions with ZIF-8 support. Therefore, the enzyme leakage from the support was much higher and enzymatic activity was lower than CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc system. In case of CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc, it appeared that the small pore openings of ZIF-8 s prevented leakage of enzyme molecules. ZIF-8 s formed protective shells around the immobilized enzyme molecules and the CQDs, which led to the exceptional stability of immobilized enzyme under harsh conditions [2]. Co-encapsulation CQDs within ZIF-8 contributed to immobilization of the enzyme molecules. As mentioned, CQDs from A. bisporus had various functionalities on the surfaces. It is probable that the CQDs served as support for the enzyme molecules and the functional moieties on the CQDs’ surface interacted with the functionalities of the enzyme molecules.

The fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solution of CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 are presented in Fig. 2. As presented in Fig. 2, CQDs from A. bisporus are fluorescent. When excited at 330 nm, the maximum emission of CQDs was recorded at 420 nm and the maximum emission of CQDs@ZIF-8 at 425 nm. When the CQDs were entrapped in ZIF-8, their fluorescence was quenched to some extent and their maximum emission shifted to slightly higher wavelength (425 nm). ZIF-8 was not fluorescent, but CQDs@ZIF-8 was fluorescent. Therefore, the blue fluorescence of CQDs@ZIF-8 can be attributed to the presence of CQDs in ZIF-8.

FT-IR spectra of ZIF-8, CQDs and CQDs@ZIF-8 are presented in Fig. 3. For C = N band of the imidazole ring, the characteristic vibrational bands of ZIF-8 and the Zn-N stretching were observed at 1567 and 420 cm−1, respectively. The sharp band at 758 cm−1 was designated to the out-of-plane bending of the aromatic ring of imidazole.

In the spectrum of the CQDs the broad vibrational band at 3378 cm−1 was ascribed to –OH stretching, 2929 cm−1 to CC (sp2) stretching, 2850 cm−1 to C–H bending, 1710 cm−1 to CC, 1628 cm−1 to CO stretching. This indicates that the oxygen bearing functionalities were bonded to the sp2 hybridized structure on the surface of CQDs [79].

The band at 2929 cm−1 was ascribed to vibrational stretching of C-H and the stretching vibration of C-H2 in both ZIF-8 and CQDs@ZIF-8. The bands at 1130 and 758 cm−1 were observed in both spectra, which is an indication of the loading of CQDs into ZIF-8. Additionally, the broad vibrational band at ~3500 cm−1 in the spectrum of CQDs@ZIF-8 can be attributed to O–H stretching [7].

The XRD diffractograms of ZIF-8, CQDs, CQDs@ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc are depicted in Fig. 3. The characteristic diffraction peaks of ZIF-8 were recorded at 7.2°, 10.6°, 12.9°, 14.9°, 16.6°, 18.2°, 19.7°, 22.3°, 24.7°, 25.4°, 26.9°, 30.8° and 31.7° (2θ). The XRD pattern of CQDs had diffraction peak at 2θ= 17°, demonstrating the amorphous character of the CQDs. On the other hand, the diffraction peaks at ~21° and 25° show the crystalline property of CQDs.

As depicted in Fig. 3, there are some differences between the diffraction patterns of ZIF-8 and CQDs@ZIF-8. Also, some peaks were shifted in case of CQDs@ZIF-8. This could be attributed to the presence of CQDs in ZIF-8 [7]. Following CRL loading, the diffraction peaks in XRD diffractogram of CQDs@ZIF-8 got weaker and broader [80].

Fig. 4 depicts the confocal microscopy images of CQDs, ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc. The blue fluorescence of the samples with carbon quantum dots i.e., CQDs, CQDs@ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc is visible by confocal microscopy. This observation demonstrated that CQDs were physically adsorbed onto ZIF-8 and entrapped in ZIF-8 [81].

Fig. 5A depicts the scanning electron microscopy images of CQDs@ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc. The surface of CQDs@ZIF-8 was smoother before the enzyme immobilization. CQDs@ZIF-8 had a uniform particle size of about 450 nm. The surface morphology of CQDs@ZIF-8 was significantly changed following the lipase immobilization. In the literature it was reported that the size and shape of the prepared MOF-biomolecule composites depended mainly on the immobilization method and the immobilized biomacromolecules [81–83]. In addition, the EDX elemental analysis of CQDs@ZIF-8 showed the presence of Zn as the component of ZIF-8 (Fig. 5B).

The TEM images of CQDs@ZIF-8, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc are given in Fig. 6. In an earlier publication the size of CQDs from A. bisporus was measured as about 20 nm [72] (Fig. 1). Since the CQDs were small enough, the CQDs entrapped in ZIF-8 did not disrupt the structure of ZIF-8. The SEM and confocal images confirmed the physical attachment of the enzyme onto ZIF-8 (CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads) and their entrapment in ZIF-8 (CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc).

The procedure followed to immobilize enzymes on insoluble supports can affect the ionization and dissociation state of the enzyme and its environment [35,66,84]. The catalytic activity of immobilized CRL (ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc) were assayed at varying pHs (4.0–9.0) ( Fig. 7A). The optimum pH was recorded at 7.0 for ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and free lipase (CRL). In other words, the physical adsorption procedure did not lead to conformational changes in lipase molecules. In the case of CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc the optimum pH was observed at slightly alkaline region (pH 8.0). The pH activity profile of CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc was broader than CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and ZIF-8 @CRL showed better pH stability [85–87].

The effect of temperature (in the range of 30–60 °C) on the activity of CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc is depicted in Fig. 7B. Both ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CRL showed the maximum catalytic activity at 35 °C. As for CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc, the maximum catalytic activity was recorded at 40 °C. This can be ascribed to the improved resistance of confined enzyme molecules to thermal denaturation and to the conformational limitations in lipase molecules entrapped within ZIF-8 [88].

In Fig. 8, the thermal stability of immobilized lipases was examined. CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc were incubated at 60 °C for 2 h and the enzymatic activity was assayed at 20-minute intervals. The CRL lost its catalytic activity completely at 60 °C after 100 min, while ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc retained 58%, 50% and 70% of their activity at the end of 120 min, respectively. It was observed that the encapsulation method effectively protected immobilized CRL against thermal denaturation.

The reusability of immobilized enzymes is very important for industrial applications. The stability of an immobilized enzyme is more advantageous compared to free enzymes. ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc were observed to retain 46%, 48% and 70% of their enzymatic activity after 6 re-uses, respectively ( Fig. 9). The lower reusability rate of CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads can be ascribed to the weak interactions between CQDs@ZIF-8 and lipase molecules i.e., hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces and hydrophobic interactions [89–92].

The storage stability of the immobilized enzymes (ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc) was examined in PBS at 4 °C for 5 weeks (There were 7-day intervals between the activity measurements) ( Fig. 10). After the five-week storage, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc activities were observed to be 51%, 50% and 73%, respectively. CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc maintained its active conformation under storage conditions. The resulting stability referred to multiple bindings of the enzyme to the support, preventing any intermolecular processes such as proteolysis and aggregation, creating a tougher enzyme molecule [85,93,94].

Michaelis-Menten kinetic parameters of free and immobilized lipases (Free CRL, ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc) were calculated ( Table 1). Free CRL showed the Km and Vmax levels of 0.44 mM and 115 (U/mg). Moreover, the ZIF-8 @CRL, CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads and CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc displayed the Km and Vmax values of 0.33 mM, 0.67 mM, 0.83 mM and 227 U/mg, 737.5 U/mg, and 453.3 U/mg, respectively. These results indicated that the affinity of CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc for the substrate was higher than those of other samples. This improvement may be due to the open conformation that makes the active site more accessible [34].

The study revealed that CQDs derived from edible mushroom A. bisporus can synergistically enhance the catalytic performance, thermal resistance, and storage stability of C. rugosa lipase entrapped within zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF-8). The in-situ encapsulation method reported here was demonstrated to be simple but effective. The prepared CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc exhibited 3.5-fold improved biocatalytic activity with respect to CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLads. It was observed that the CQDs@ZIF-8 @CRLenc showed an excellent recycle performance that can retain 70% of its initial activity even after being reused 6 times. Once it is entrapped within ZIF-8 crystals, the lipase-CQDs system can be tested in various applications. In further studies, a similar approach can be adopted for industrial enzymes other than lipase. In this study CQDs synthesized from A. bisporus mushroom via microwave irradiation were used. Since properties of CQDs are largely governed by the carbonization precursor and the method (hydrothermal, solvothermal, pyrolysis, microwave treatment etc.) used in the synthesis, CQDs from various sources should also be examined in ZIF-8 or other metal–organic frameworks (MOFs). Production of CQDs should be encouraged due to its cost-effectiveness, wide availability, low toxicity and eco-friendliness. Besides that, this study evaluated enzyme immobilization by using CQDs due to their low toxicity, strong fluorescent effect, tunable emission and excellent biocompatibility. Therefore, more fundamental researches and in-depth studies on the biocatalytic properties of the CQDs-MOFs composites as biocatalyst should be expected in the near future.
